name: Neapolitan
description: The original: a soft, blistered cornicione, a thin center under San Marzano tomatoes and fior di latte, and 60 to 90 seconds in a wood oven running close to 485C.

name: Neapolitan Contemporanea
description: Modern Neapolitan pizza that keeps the DNA but rewrites the rim: higher hydration, longer ferments and a tall, airy canotto crust, built for lightness.

name: Pizza al Taglio
description: Rome's by-the-slice tradition: baked in long rectangular trays, sold by weight and cut with scissors, with a high-hydration, long-fermented open crumb.

name: Pizza alla Pala
description: The Roman cousin of al taglio, baked and served on a long wooden peel in an elongated oval, with very wet, long-fermented dough and a light, crisp crust.

name: Roman Tonda
description: Rolled paper-thin and baked until the base shatters like a cracker, with almost no raised rim. That scrocchiarella snap is the whole appeal.

name: Pinsa
description: The oval, feather-light Roman flatbread: wheat blended with rice and soy flours, very high hydration and a long cold ferment, crisp outside and airy inside.

name: Pizza al Padellino
description: Turin's small, personal pan pizza, also called al tegamino: thick and soft in the middle with a bottom fried crisp by the oiled pan.

name: Sicilian
description: Thick, square and built for the bakery, with roots in sfincione, a spongy Palermo flatbread topped with onion, breadcrumb, anchovy and caciocavallo.

name: Pizza Fritta
description: Naples' fried answer to the oven: dough stuffed with ricotta, cicoli, provola and a little tomato, sealed and dropped into hot oil until it puffs gold.

name: New York-Style
description: Wide, thin, hand-tossed and foldable, baked in a hot deck oven until the underside is spotted and pliable. Descended from Neapolitan, stretched larger.

name: New Haven Apizza
description: Connecticut's coal-fired pizza: thin, irregular, charred hard at the edges, with a dry, smoky, chewy crust closer to its Neapolitan ancestor than to New York.

name: Detroit-Style
description: The rectangular one with crunchy, lacy edges, baked in blue-steel pans with brick cheese pushed to the walls so it fries into a crisp frico rim, sauce on last.

name: Chicago Deep-Dish
description: Built in a high-edged pan and stacked in reverse: cheese on the dough, fillings next, and crushed tomato on top so the long bake does not scorch it.
